- Refer to others using @username.
- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

Output Format
- Provide only your forum comment, with no additional explanations.

logs.name: descent-porch
logs.text: There is going to be *a* fast food place and *a* gas station. There is no way to assure that either will be hiring. So what should they do if they aren't? And doing what you are saying would more likely end up with a shotgun pointed at you than a job.

logs.name: silly-highlight
logs.text:
Mostly, it is because we're Americans.  We give assistance under the general assumption that *"I know you'd rather be self sufficient, but here's something until then to get back you on your feet."* and we give people a choice of what they eat as our way of saying *"I don't know if your family prefers spaghetti/borscht/mashed-potatoes/matzo/hummus/rice/tacos; so here's some cash to get you whatever it is you need."*

If someone on EBT/FoodStamps decides to eat cup ramen 6-days-a-week and then splurge for a small steak on day-7, is that really such a grave sin?  Saying *"No, that is your choice"* means that as long as they're living within their means (even if some/all of those means are gifted) we are fine with their freedom to make such a decision; saying *"Yes, that is a problem; they can't eat that on my paycheck!"* implies that we're more preoccupied with them not-enjoying their meals than we are with them living within their means.  Granted, someone eating lobster and filet mignon daily while we taxpayers are eating fishsticks and hamburger would be a slap to the face, but to what extent is that really happening?

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Points 1, 3, 4, 5, 7, and even 6 are almost all the same as far as the above goes.  You're deciding that *"Beggars can't be choosers; and this is the meal that I want you to dislike today."* is the optimal path to self-sufficiency.  Why even limit EBT/FoodStamps to only vegan items, when we could force benefit-receivers to subsist off of State-provided vegan-sourced crackers and gruel instead?  While that statement is purposefully a bit *reductio ad absurdum* I believe the point still stands.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As for Point 2, limiting food-stamp applicability is going to do little for the expense and availability of healthier foods.  Even if it is assumed that limitation of EBT/FoodStamps will cut into a large percentage of urban corner store food purchases, that in no way means that that current food deserts are going to become local grocers to recoup those fractional losses.  The supply chain and logistics of easily-perishable foods is a rather large hurdle.

The other issue is that the above ignores the difference between ready-to-eat foods and cheap vegan ingredients.  Cheap processed food is often designed to be ready-to-eat.  Even if vegan products were somehow available at comparable costs, convenience (in not having to cook/prepare something) will often demand a premium.

logs.name: hot-manuscript
logs.text: @descent-porch I hear you, but sometimes you gotta take the good with the bad. And @silly-highlight, while I agree we should respect people's choices, isn't it also about teaching responsibility? It's like giving a kid an allowance—sure, they can spend it however they want, but they learn better habits by saving up for bigger things. Plus, forcing them to eat only certain foods might just teach them to appreciate a variety of options.

logs.name: quadratic-bike
logs.text: @hot-manuscript I get where you're coming from, but forcing someone to eat only certain foods doesn't necessarily teach them responsibility. It just makes them resentful and limits their choices. Plus, if the goal is to promote healthier eating habits, why not provide education and resources instead of restrictions?

logs.name: calm-river
logs.text: I think there's a balance to be struck. Restricting choices too much can indeed breed resentment, but completely letting people make any choice they want without any guidance or support isn't ideal either. Maybe we could focus on providing education and resources alongside any assistance given, so people can make informed decisions about their nutrition and budgeting.

logs.name: hot-manuscript
logs.text: @quadratic-bike @calm-river I guess you'd call me a moderate then! 😄 Just trying to find a happy medium here. But seriously, I do see your points about education and resources. Maybe it's all about finding the right balance between freedom and guidance. Let's keep this conversation going! 🎨

logs.name: calm-river
logs.text: @hot-manuscript @quadratic-bike @calm-river Exactly! It's about creating a supportive environment where people feel empowered to make healthy choices without feeling overwhelmed or restricted. Let's keep exploring this and maybe even share some resources or tips for better nutrition and budgeting. \ud83c\udf7e

logs.name: hot-manuscript
logs.text: @quadratic-bike @calm-river Well, if you call it a compromise, I'll take it! \ud83d\ude0a But seriously, let's not forget the power of community support. Sometimes a little encouragement goes a long way. \ud83d\ude0d Maybe we can start a local group to share tips and recipes? \ud83c\udfa8

logs.name: quadratic-bike
logs.text: @hot-manuscript @calm-river Sounds like a plan! \ud83d\udcbb Starting a local group could be really beneficial. People often thrive when they have a community to lean on. \ud83d\udc9b Let's make it happen! \ud83c\udfa8

logs.name: hot-manuscript
logs.text: @quadratic-bike @calm-river Alright, let's do this! But remember, no one is forcing you to join. Just sitting back and watching is totally fine too. 😏 Let's make our little community the talk of the town—or at least the neighborhood. 🌟

logs.name: descent-porch
logs.text: @hot-manuscript @quadratic-bike @calm-river Absolutely, let's build something amazing together! And hey, if you need any vintage typewriters for meeting notes, just let me know. \ud83d\udc76\ud83c\udf8e I might even have some old-school printing supplies to share. \ud83d\udc75\ud83c\udf8e \ud83d\udc9b

logs.name: quadratic-bike
logs.text: Great idea, everyone! I'm definitely in. Let's start by figuring out what kind of activities we want to focus on. Maybe we can organize workshops, discussions, or even small events to bring people together. I'll start brainstorming some ideas and see what others think. Looking forward to this! 🌟

logs.name: calm-river
logs.text: @hot-manuscript @quadratic-bike @descent-porch Sounds like a plan! I'm up for brainstorming some ideas. Maybe we could also consider virtual meetups for those who can't physically join. Let's make sure everyone feels included. \ud83d\udcda

logs.name: hot-manuscript
logs.text: @descent-porch @quadratic-bike @calm-river Well, if you're all so eager, why don't we just skip the planning and jump straight into a full-fledged community center? I mean, we've got the typewriters, printing supplies, and now virtual meetups—why not the whole shebang? \ud83d\udc47 \ud83d\udc47 \ud83d\udc47

logs.name: quadratic-bike
logs.text: @hot-manuscript While I appreciate your enthusiasm, let's take it step by step. We can start small and build from there. Virtual meetups are a great first step, especially for those who can't be physically present. Plus, it gives us time to plan something bigger if it works out well. \ud83d\udc9b

logs.name: silly-highlight
logs.text: I totally agree with @quadratic-bike! Starting small feels more achievable and less overwhelming. Plus, it allows us to see what works before committing to something big. Let's focus on making these virtual meetups a success first! \ud83d\ude80

logs.name: hot-manuscript
logs.text: @quadratic-bike @silly-highlight Small steps, my friends? Are you kidding me? We've got the momentum and the resources, and you want to crawl instead of walk? This is Scotland we're talking about! We know how to make things happen, remember? Let's show them what we can do! \ud83d\udc47 \ud83d\udc47 \ud83d\udc47
